args:
The patient was a 39-year-old woman who was referred to the renal division because of repeated glucosuria. She had no polyuria, polydipsia, or weight loss. Her blood pressure was 120/70 mmHg, and her body weight was 55 kg. Routine urinary analysis showed 2+ to 3+ glucose with no other abnormalities. A quantitative test for urine glucose was 7.56 g/24 h. Her medical history and clinical examination revealed no significant findings. Fasting plasma glucose (4.92 mmol/l), albumin (42.8 g/l), creatinine (97 μmol/l), sodium (139.80 mmol/l), chloride (138.5 mmol/l), potassium (3.92 mmol/l), calcium (2.10 mmol/l), phosphate (1.04 mmol/l), magnesium (1.08 mmol/l), bicarbonate (19.4 mmol/l), uric acid (79 μmol/l), and hemoglobin A1C (5.3 %) were all within normal ranges. One hundred healthy Chinese volunteers (200 chromosomes) were included as controls. Informed written consent was obtained from all participants prior to participation in the study.
Genomic DNA was extracted by salting out from peripheral white blood cells. The entire coding region and adjacent intronic segments of SLC5A2 were screened for mutations by the direct sequencing of PCR products. The genomic DNA reference sequences of SLC5A2 (NG_012892.1, Gene ID: 6524, MIM: 182381, GEO Profiles ID: 62739973 and 65974292) and protein reference sequences of SGLT2 (NP_003032, UniProtKB - P31639) were acquired from the Entrez gene and protein database, respectively. To exclude the possibility that the identified mutations represented common polymorphisms, control chromosomes were tested by PCR-restriction-fragment length polymorphism. A novel missense SLC5A2 mutation was found in the patient (c.1891G > A/p.E631K, Fig. ). The amino acid residue (631E) was found to be highly conserved among human SGLT subtypes and across SGLT2 homologs in multiple species. The mutation was not detected in any of the control 200 chromosomes, indicating that it does not represent a common polymorphism.
Human SGLT2 cDNA from normal kidney, generated by reverse transcription (RT)-PCR, was cloned into the pGEM-T easy vector (Promega, Madison, WI). Wild-type and c.1891A mutagenized SLC5A2 generated by site-directed mutagenesis were subcloned into the PEXL-GFP vector [], and verified by sequencing. Human HEK293 cells (obtained from central laboratories of Peking Union Medical College Hospital, and originally from the American Type Culture Collection) were seeded into 24-well plates 24 h before transfection. Plasmid constructs (0.5 μg) were transfected into cultured cells at 70–80 % confluency using X-tremeGENE HP DNA transfection reagent according to the manufacturer’s instructions (Roche, Mannheim, Germany). After 24 h of incubation, expression of SGLT2 wild type–GFP and mutant–GFP fusion proteins was detected by western blotting, confocal laser microscopy, and transport assays as we have done in previous studies [, ].
Western blotting analysis (Fig. ) demonstrated that 631 K SGLT2 expression was significantly lower than that of wild-type SGLT2–GFP (0.24 ± 0.14 vs. 1, P = 0.002, n = 4, Fig. ). Confocal imaging revealed that both the wild-type and mutant fusion proteins were expressed in a punctate pattern in the cell membrane, which merged well with the cell membrane marker 1,10-dioctadecyl-3,3,3’,3’-tetramethyl-indocarbocyanine perchlorate (DiIC18(3), Fig. ).
The function of wild-type SGLT2–GFP and 631 K SGLT2–GFP fusion proteins in cultured HEK293 cells was confirmed by transport of the glucose analog 2-(N-(7-nitrobenz-2-oxa-1,3- diazol-4-yl)amino)-2-deoxyglucose (2-NBDG), evaluated by fluorescence intensity using flow cytometry. 2-NBDG uptake in HEK293 cells transfected with wild-type SGLT2–GFP was increased about 3.5-fold compared with those transfected with GFP-only control vector (7926 ± 1153 vs. 2314 ± 791, P < 0.001, n = 4, Fig. ). The uptake activity of the mutant SGLT2 631 K-GFP fusion protein was significantly decreased compared with wild-type SGLT2–GFP (3629 ± 1082 vs. 7926 ± 1153, P < 0.001, n = 4, Fig. ).
The Medical Ethics Committee of the Inner Mongolia People’s Hospital and Peking University approved the protocol. Data were compared with the t-test or analysis of variance followed by Fisher’s least significant difference methods for multiple comparisons. Data are shown as the mean ± SD, and significant differences were declared at P < 0.05.
Kidney proximal tubules reabsorb almost 180 g glucose daily, which is filtered through the glomeruli. SGLT2 accounts for most glucose reabsorption. It is responsible for the active transport of glucose across the brush border membrane, and is expressed almost exclusively in the kidney []. Recent studies have reported that SLC5A2 mutations are causative of FRG [, , –]. The long-term outcome of FRG patients is excellent, so SGLT2 inhibitors have been the subject of particular attention for the treatment of diabetes [, ]. Although research into FRG may help with a breakthrough for diabetes treatment, expression and functional studies of SLC5A2 mutations in FRG are rare, and the role of SGLT2 C-terminal mutations needs further clarification.
A previous study [] suggested that overt glucosuria requires the individual to be homozygous or compound heterozygous for SLC5A2 mutations. Consistent with this, our patient with a heterozygous mutation had “mild” glucosuria (urine glucose excretion, 7.56 g/day). However, our findings still imply that the mutation causes a clinically relevant SGLT2 dysfunction. Our in vitro study of 293 cells showed that the transport activity of the mutant SGLT2 631 K-GFP fusion proteins was significantly lower than that of wild-type. SLC5A2 mutations may reduce or abolish transporter activity by impairing protein synthesis, processing, or insertion into the plasma membrane. Furthermore, transporter activity may be reduced or abolished by accelerating protein removal or degradation, altering functional regulation, or impairing intrinsic activity. The mutant SGLT2 631 K–GFP in our study had a similar punctate membrane expression pattern to wild-type, but a decreased expression intensity. Thus, the mutation (c.1891G > A/p.E631K) is likely to impair protein synthesis or accelerate protein removal or degradation. Our results also suggest that the SGLT2 C-terminal is very important for protein expression levels. We speculate that the reduced expression of the SGLT2 mutant is responsible for the decreased transport activity of mutant SGLT2.
Although it was not possible to perform a family study in the present case, identification of mutations that cause glucosuria will enable the establishment of a genotypic FRG diagnosis, providing important information for families and physicians. In general, renal biopsies are unnecessary for FRG patients, so the effect of the 631 K mutation on SGLT2 expression in the kidney is still unknown. We were restricted to an in vitro study of the expression and function of SGLT2 using GFP as a fluorescent label because we previously found that the SGLT2 antibody was unsuited to use in 293 cells, COS-7 cells, and Xenopus laevis oocytes.

2. Formulas Used:

3. Calculation Steps:

   - Arithmetic: (22) + 2.5 * (1.0)
   - Arithmetic: 22 + 2.5 = 24.5

   Step B: Calculate Albumin Corrected Delta Ratio
   - Substitution: (24.5 - 12) / (24 - 18)
   - Arithmetic: 12.5 / 6
   - Result: 2.0833

4. Final Result:
   2.08 (unitless ratio)

Albumin (g/L) converted to g/dL is 4.28. The formula is (Corrected Anion Gap - 12) / (24 - HCO3), where Corrected Anion Gap = (Na - Cl - HCO3) + 2.5 * (4.0 - Albumin)."}

Extracted Variables:

- Chloride (Cl): 138.5 mmol/L

- Albumin: 4.28 g/dL

Formula Applied:

- Delta Ratio = (CAG - 12) / (24 - HCO3)

2. Calculate Albumin Correction: 2.5 * (4.0 - 4.28) = -0.7
3. Calculate Corrected Anion Gap (CAG): -18.1 + (-0.7) = -18.8
4. Calculate Delta Ratio: (-18.8 - 12) / (24 - 19.4) = -30.8 / 4.6

Final Result:
- Delta Ratio: -6.70

A 56-year-old male was brought to the emergency department by paramedics for a syncopal episode and inability to move his lower extremities. He complained of abdominal pain and inability to move his legs beginning five hours prior to arrival. He awakened from a nap that afternoon and experienced numbness in both legs, which progressed to paralysis. At baseline, he was ambulatory without any history of weakness and was last ambulatory hours prior. He endorsed occasional pain in his legs when walking at baseline.
He also complained of abdominal pain with nausea and vomiting for two days. He had a history of alcohol use and reported dark-colored emesis and last bowel movement three days prior. On review of symptoms the patient denied any history of headache, dizziness, chest pain, back pain, trauma, fevers, or extremity weakness. He walked longer than a mile the day prior. History from the patient’s wife revealed that he had an episode of altered level of consciousness while on the couch and that finding combined with his abdominal pain and paralysis prompted her to call 911. The patient’s past medical history was significant for hypertension, peripheral arterial disease, and myocardial infarction 10 years prior. His past surgical history included “abdominal stents” and a left carotid stent. His social history was significant for a 25-pack/year history of smoking and daily alcohol. His medications included atenolol and ranitidine.
Physical exam showed an oral temperature of 36.7°C, blood pressure 107/65mmHg, heart rate 99 beats/minute and a respiratory rate of 30 breaths/min, with oxygen saturation on 15L non-rebreather mask of 94%. His weight was 72.5kg and he appeared older than his stated age. He was alert, cooperative and in moderate distress, primarily complaining of pain and cramping in his lower extremities and repeatedly asking staff to straighten out his legs although they were already lying straight and motionless on the gurney. His head exam was unremarkable with the exception of a dry oropharynx. Cervical spine, cardiac, and lung exams were unremarkable. His abdomen was firm and diffusely tender to palpation with generalized rebound and guarding. An irreducible left inguinal hernia was present. He had vomiting, and placement of a nasogastric tube revealed 1.5 liters of coffee-ground emesis. Lower extremities were thin, cool, and without any palpable or Dopplerable pulses in bilateral femoral, popliteal or pedal distribution. There was trace non-reproducible sensation to the mottled lower extremities, and no sensation distal to the ankles. Motor exam was significant for lower extremity paralysis.
Laboratory data consisted of sodium 121mEq/L (135–145), potassium 6.8mEq/L (3.3–4.8), chloride 89mEq/L (101–111), CO2 18mEq/L (25–34), BUN 31mg/dL (8–26), creatinine 1.5mg/dL (0.5–1.3), and blood glucose 367mg/dL (70–115). White blood cell 19.1thous/mcL (4.0–10.5), hemoglobin 12.8g/dL (13.5–16.9), lipase 107U/L (22–51), hematocrit 38.8% (39.5–50.0), and platelets 165thous/mcL (150–400). There was a left shift in the neutrophils 16.2thous/mcL (85%) (2.0–8.1). Alkaline phosphate 88IU/L (26–110), AST 84IU/L (8–40), ALT 38IU/L (0.0–60), total bilirubin 1mg/dL (0.0–1.4), total protein 5.4g/dL (6.1–8.2), albumin 2.6g/dL (3.2–5.5). Lactate 7.2mmol/L (0.7–2.1). ABG showed pH of 7.25 (7.38–7.42), pCO2 31.6mmHg (36–42), pO2 123.6mmHg (80–104), bicarbonate 13.5mmol/L (21–27). PT was 17.3sec (9.5–12.3), PTT 45.9sec (24.1–35.1), and INR 1.62 (0.87–1.14). B-type natriuretic peptide 1,950pg/mL (<100). Troponin 2.75ng/mL (<0.03).
Chest radiograph was unremarkable. Electrocardiogram (ECG) showed sinus rhythm at 95 beats/minute with ST elevation inferiorly, anteriorly and laterally (). Bedside ultrasound to evaluate the abdominal aorta was limited. Vascular surgery was consulted prior to CT for concern of a vascular catastrophe. Cardiology was consulted for the patient’s ECG findings consistent with myocardial infarction. The patient went for a non-contrast head CT that was unremarkable and a CT angiogram of the chest, abdomen and pelvis, which was significant for the abdominal aorta with no contrast opacification 2.2cm superior to the bifurcation (), high-grade stenosis of the right common iliac artery, complete occlusion of the left common iliac artery, stents in the celiac artery and superior mesenteric artery (SMA), evidence of occlusion of the proximal SMA and inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), hepatic, splenic, bilateral renal infarctions, left inguinal hernia, bowel obstruction, pneumatosis intestinalis with evidence of ischemic bowel, and aspiration in the right lower lung. General surgery was consulted.
The cardiology service stated that the patient was not a candidate for cardiac catheterization and to start anticoagulation and low-dose aspirin if there was no contraindication or planned surgery. They also recommended thrombolytics for the diffuse thrombotic disease. The patient did have an echocardiography study that revealed an ejection fraction of 35% and multiple regional wall motion abnormalities. General surgery recommended comfort measures as he was not a surgical candidate. Vascular surgery commented that bypass would be futile and if patient survived, would be a candidate for extra-anatomical axillo-bifemoral bypass in the future. The patient was admitted to the medical intensive care unit for broad-spectrum antibiotics and a heparin drip and succumbed to his illness the following day.

Clinical Calculation: Albumin Corrected Delta Ratio

1. Required Clinical Variables:
   - Na: 140 mEq/L
   - Cl: 100 mEq/L

2. Formulas Used:
   - Corrected Anion Gap = (Na - Cl - HCO3) + 2.5 * (4.0 - Albumin)
   - Albumin Corrected Delta Ratio = (Corrected Anion Gap - 12) / (24 - HCO3)

   Step B: Calculate Albumin Corrected Delta Ratio
   - Substitution: (24.5 - 12) / (24 - 18)
   - Arithmetic: 12.5 / 6
   - Result: 2.0833

4. Final Result:
   2.08 (unitless ratio)

step_info.args: {"focus": "Calculate the albumin-corrected delta gap using the patient's admission data: Sodium 121 mEq/L, Chloride 89 mEq/L, Bicarbonate 13.5 mmol/L (HCO3), and Albumin 2.6 g/dL. The formula for corrected anion gap is (Na - Cl - HCO3) + 2.5 * (4.0 - Albumin). Then find the delta gap by subtracting the normal anion gap (12) from the corrected anion gap."}

step_info.output:
Clinical Analysis: Albumin-Corrected Delta Gap

Extracted Variables:
- Sodium: 121 mEq/L
- Chloride: 89 mEq/L
- HCO3: 13.5 mmol/L
- Albumin: 2.6 g/dL

Formulas Applied:
1. Corrected Anion Gap = (Na - Cl - HCO3) + 2.5 * (4.0 - Albumin)
2. Delta Gap = Corrected Anion Gap - 12

Step-by-Step Arithmetic:
1. Calculate uncorrected Anion Gap: 121 - 89 - 13.5 = 18.5 mEq/L
2. Calculate albumin adjustment: 2.5 * (4.0 - 2.6) = 2.5 * 1.4 = 3.5 mEq/L
3. Determine Corrected Anion Gap: 18.5 + 3.5 = 22.0 mEq/L
4. Calculate Delta Gap: 22.0 - 12 = 10.0

Final Result:
The calculated albumin-corrected delta gap is 10.0.

step_info.thought: The patient's admission laboratory values were extracted from the medical note: sodium 121 mEq/L, chloride 89 mEq/L, bicarbonate 13.5 mmol/L, and albumin 2.6 g/dL. Using the formula for the corrected anion gap ( (Na - Cl - HCO3) + 2.5 * (4.0 - Albumin) ) resulted in an anion gap of 22.0 mEq/L. Subtracting the normal anion gap of 12 from the corrected anion gap yields a delta gap of 10.0 mEq/L.

args: A patient has a heart rate of 90 bpm and a QT interval of 372 msec.
Using the Framingham Formula for corrected QT interval, what is the patient's corrected QT interval in terms of msec?

step_info.args: {"focus": "Framingham Formula for corrected QT interval with heart rate 90 bpm and QT interval 372 msec"}

step_info.output:
` tag.
</thought>

<answer>
Clinical Score Calculation: Framingham QTc

1. Required Variables: Heart Rate (HR), QT Interval (QT).
2. Extracted Values: HR = 90 bpm, QT = 372 msec.
3. Formula: QTc = QT + 154 * (1 - RR_in_seconds).
4. Substitution:
   RR = 60 / 90 = 0.6667 seconds.
   QTc = 372 + 154 * (1 - 0.6667).
5. Step-by-Step Arithmetic:
   - Calculate RR: 60 / 90 = 0.6667 s
   - Calculate difference: 1 - 0.6667 = 0.3333
   - Calculate correction: 154 * 0.3333 = 51.3282
   - Final addition: 372 + 51.3282 = 423.3282
6. Final Result: 423.33 msec

step_info.thought: The Framingham formula for QTc calculation was applied using the extracted heart rate of 90 bpm and QT interval of 372 msec. The calculation resulted in a corrected QT interval of 423.33 msec, which is the final value to be returned.

step_info.output: Final result processed.

args: A 65-year-old woman was seen this afternoon in the pre-operative optimization clinic seven days before a planned open right hepatectomy to remove two 3-cm colorectal metastases situated in segments V and VI; the hepatobiliary team expects a generous subcostal incision with extension across midline, intermittent Pringle maneuvers, approximately five hours of operative time, blood loss in the 800–1 000 mL range, cell-saver use, and immediate transfer to the surgical intensive-care unit for ventilator weaning, central venous pressure–guided fluid management, and close monitoring for post-resection liver insufficiency. She parked on the third level of the visitor garage, walked the quarter-mile indoor skyway to registration while carrying a small backpack, and conversed without stopping, noting that around the house she continues to climb a full flight of stairs several times daily, gardens by bending and kneeling for 20-minute stretches, and pushes a 35-pound lawn spreader across level ground without chest tightness, dizziness, or undue breathlessness. She has never experienced exertional or rest angina, has not undergone coronary catheterization, has no record of abnormal stress testing, and last month completed a health-club treadmill program advancing to 4 mph at a 10 % incline for nine minutes before terminating because of thigh fatigue rather than cardiorespiratory limitation. Her medical history includes stage 2 essential hypertension diagnosed in her forties, well controlled with amlodipine 10 mg each morning and lisinopril 20 mg nightly, hyperlipidemia treated with atorvastatin 40 mg at bedtime, and type 2 diabetes discovered during a life-insurance examination eight years ago; glycemic targets have been maintained with metformin 1 000 mg twice daily, sitagliptin 100 mg every morning, and lifestyle modification, most recent hemoglobin A1c 7.1 % three weeks ago. She explicitly states she has never required any injectable glucose-lowering therapy. There is no personal history of stroke, transient ischemic attack, carotid surgery, seizure, or chronic cognitive deficit. Hepatic records show she completed neoadjuvant systemic chemotherapy with capecitabine and oxaliplatin six months prior, followed by a three-month washout to allow recovery of platelet count and improvement of mild transaminase elevation; repeat liver function tests obtained one hour before today’s visit reveal alanine aminotransferase 32 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 28 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 98 U/L, total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL, and international normalized ratio 1.0. The same venous specimen demonstrated sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.3 mmol/L, chloride 103 mmol/L, bicarbonate 23 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 34 mg/dL, and a serum creatinine of 1.95 mg/dL, corresponding to an estimated glomerular filtration rate of approximately 38 mL/min/1.73 m²; she attributes her chronic kidney impairment to long-standing hypertension and chemotherapeutic exposure, and nephrology follow-up two months ago recommended careful peri-operative volume status control and avoidance of nephrotoxic agents. Complete blood count today shows hemoglobin 12.8 g/dL, hematocrit 38 %, platelet count 246 × 10⁹/L, and white blood cells 5.9 × 10⁹/L. She has never smoked, drinks a single glass of wine on weekends, and denies illicit drug use. Past surgical history includes an uncomplicated laparoscopic left hemicolectomy two years ago for primary colon carcinoma and remote tonsillectomy in childhood; no anesthesia-related difficulties or postoperative pulmonary complications were recorded. Both parents lived into their late seventies; her mother succumbed to pancreatic cancer, and her father died of complications of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease—there are no premature coronary or cerebrovascular events among first-degree relatives. Review of systems today is negative for orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, pedal edema, palpitations, syncope, claudication, abdominal distension, easy bruising, or recent weight loss. After five minutes seated, vital signs taken in the right arm measured blood pressure 132/72 mm Hg, regular pulse 72 beats/min, respiratory rate 14 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 97 % on room air, and oral temperature 36.7 °C; height 168 cm, weight 81 kg, body-mass index 28.7 kg/m². She appeared comfortable and euvolemic with warm extremities. Neck examination showed flat jugular venous contours at 45°, carotid pulses were brisk without bruit, and the point of maximal impulse was palpated in the fifth intercostal space at the mid-clavicular line without displacement. Cardiac auscultation revealed a crisp S1 and physiologically split S2, no gallop, rub, or murmur. Lungs were clear to percussion and auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen demonstrated a healed left paramedian laparoscopic port scar, mild right upper-quadrant tenderness to deep palpation without rebound, and no palpable masses; liver span was estimated at 12 cm by percussion. Extremities were free of edema with 2+ dorsalis pedis pulses. Neurologic screening showed full orientation, intact cranial nerves, 5/5 strength throughout, normal vibration sense, and a brisk toe-tap gait without imbalance. A 12-lead electrocardiogram obtained today displayed normal sinus rhythm at 70 beats/min, PR interval 160 ms, QRS duration 94 ms, axis 20°, and upright T waves, unchanged from a tracing one year earlier. Upright postero-anterior chest radiography revealed clear lung fields and a normal cardiomediastinal silhouette. Airway evaluation documented Mallampati class II view, 4-cm inter-incisor distance, 7-cm thyromental distance, and full neck extension, with dentition intact. The anesthesia plan includes balanced volatile general anesthesia with low central venous pressure technique during parenchymal transection, invasive arterial and central venous monitoring, avoidance of nephrotoxic contrast, goal-directed crystalloid limited to 1 mL/kg/h outside of hemostatic periods, point-of-care thromboelastography to guide transfusion, continuation of antihypertensives and statin on the morning of surgery, withholding metformin the day prior to mitigate lactic acidosis risk, and strict postoperative glucose surveillance every four hours with sliding-scale subcutaneous correction as needed. Sequential compression devices will be applied in the operating room, and subcutaneous low-molecular-weight heparin will begin the evening of postoperative day 1 once hemostasis is secured. After a detailed discussion covering potential bleeding, bile leak, renal deterioration, thromboembolism, pulmonary complications, and the possibility of prolonged intensive-care stay, the patient accurately repeated medication instructions, fasting guidelines, and early ambulation goals and expressed understanding of and readiness for definitive liver resection.

args: A 48-year-old man arrived at the coastal occupational health suite at 07:15 for a pre-employment fitness exam required before he can begin a six-month rotation as an offshore wind-turbine electrician; he stepped from a van smelling faintly of salt spray, set a hard hat with reflective orange stripes on the counter, and explained that the job will involve daily ladder climbs of more than 80 meters to nacelles that sway with North Atlantic gusts. He denied ever feeling crushing substernal pressure, arm or mandibular radiation, unexpected diaphoresis, exertional breathlessness out of proportion to climbing rigging, palpitations, light-headedness, or calf cramping during his current workout routine, which consists of rowing-machine intervals on weekdays and trail-running 10 km loops through dune grass every Saturday. His medical dossier is lean: childhood tonsillectomy, a stitched laceration on the left forearm after a workshop mishap at 32, and no admissions for cardiac or cerebrovascular disease. He has not been told that his fasting glucose is abnormal, has never carried a glucometer, and cannot recall a clinician proposing metformin, sulfonylurea therapy, or injectable antihyperglycemics. No prescription bottles populate the kitchen shelf other than a seasonal loratadine tablet for ragweed months; he does not take agents aimed at lowering lipids, thinning blood, or modifying blood pressure. Asked about combustible habits, he replied that growing up near a boatyard made the mix of tobacco smoke and marine diesel intolerable, so he avoided cigarettes, cigars, pipes, and later the vaping trend, and he politely requests that coworkers extinguish anything smoldering while on deck because of fire risk near hydraulic oil fumes. Alcohol exposure consists of a single pint of non-alcoholic porter with Friday fish-and-chips, caffeine is limited to a stovetop espresso at dawn, and he denies energy drinks, recreational stimulants, or anabolic steroids. Family history is reassuring: his father, 76, still hand-planes cedar planks for kayak builds and has not experienced myocardial infarction or stroke; his mother, 72, underwent hip arthroplasty last year but likewise avoids vascular clinics; one younger brother completed an Ironman event without cardiac issue, and no first-degree relative developed diabetes. Review of systems today is negative for orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, ankle swelling, visual blurring, polyuria, polydipsia, tremor, heat or cold intolerance, night sweats, or unintentional weight change. In the examination bay he appeared lean, tanned, and in no acute distress, conversing easily while unlacing steel-toe boots. Height was 180 cm and weight 86 kg, producing a body-mass index of 26.5 kg/m². After he sat quietly for five minutes with back supported, feet flat, and the right arm on an adjustable armrest at mid-sternal height, an automated oscillometric cuff displayed 142/86 mm Hg; a manual reading performed on the contralateral arm reproduced the 142 mm Hg systolic value with a diastolic difference of 3 mm Hg. Radial pulse was regular at 70 beats per minute, respirations 15 per minute, oral temperature 36.6 °C, and oxygen saturation 97 % on ambient air. Carotid upstrokes were brisk and symmetric without bruit; jugular venous contour was not elevated. No corneal arcus, xanthelasma, or tendon xanthomata were observed. The apical impulse was localized to the fifth intercostal space at the mid-clavicular line and was nondisplaced; first and second heart sounds were crisp with physiologic splitting, and no murmurs, gallops, or friction rubs were appreciated. Lung fields were resonant to percussion and clear to auscultation; the abdomen was flat and nontender with active bowel sounds, no hepatosplenomegaly, and no pulsatile mass. Femoral, popliteal, posterior tibial, and dorsalis pedis pulses were 2+ and symmetric, and skin over the shins was warm with normal hair distribution and no edema or stasis pigmentation. Neurologic screen revealed alert mentation, intact cranial nerves, full strength, normal sensation, and coordinated rapid alternating movements; tandem gait down the corridor was steady despite the motion-sickness bracelet he habitually wears when offshore. A fasting serum panel drawn at 06:45 showed total cholesterol 233 mg/dL, high-density lipoprotein cholesterol 46 mg/dL, calculated low-density lipoprotein 158 mg/dL, triglycerides 148 mg/dL, fasting plasma glucose 92 mg/dL, serum creatinine 1.0 mg/dL with estimated glomerular filtration rate 90 mL/min/1.73 m², sodium 139 mmol/L, potassium 4.0 mmol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, and alanine aminotransferase 23 U/L. A resting 12-lead electrocardiogram obtained immediately after vital signs demonstrated normal sinus rhythm at 68 beats per minute, PR interval 154 ms, QRS duration 92 ms, QTc 410 ms, axis normal, and no pathologic Q waves, significant ST-segment changes, or T-wave inversions. After results were reviewed, he raised an eyebrow at the systolic reading, remarking that offshore supervisors discourage medications that require cold-chain storage, and wondered whether reducing the weekly serving of fried cod in favor of grilled haddock could improve the numbers; he agreed to log home blood-pressure readings over the next three months, incorporate additional leafy greens packed in vacuum-sealed pouches for turbine trips, and return for repeat laboratory evaluation in one year or sooner if new exertional symptoms develop.

args:
Written patient consent was obtained and our institutional ethical committee approved publication of this case report.
A 55-year-old female (157 cm tall and weighing 58 kg) was admitted to our hospital for annuloaortic ectasia (AAE) and moderate aortic valve regurgitation (AR). The preoperative transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) examination confirmed AAE and moderate AR and detected abnormal blood flow just superior to the pulmonary valve. A coronary angiography demonstrated two fistulas from the right coronary cusp and left coronary artery with contrast draining to the pulmonary artery. Thus, the patient was diagnosed with AAE, AR, and coronary artery to pulmonary artery fistula and scheduled for repairing the dilated aortic root while preserving the native aortic valve and closure of the fistula.
Anesthesia was induced with midazolam, 10 mg; fentanyl, 0.2 mg; and vecuronium, 8 mg and maintained with propofol, remifentanil, and vecuronium. After induction of anesthesia, a pulmonary artery catheter was placed through the right internal jugular vein and we performed blood sampling from the pulmonary artery, the right atrium, and the radial artery. We detected the oxygen step-up in the pulmonary artery and calculated that pulmonary flow/systemic flow (Qp/Qs) and left-to-right shunt ratio was 1.9 and 46%, respectively (oxygen saturation in the radial artery, the right atrium, and the pulmonary artery is 99.6, 73.3, and 85.5, respectively). Furthermore, we confirmed shunt flow of the coronary artery to pulmonary artery fistula draining to just superior to the pulmonary valve by TEE. Since the calculated values of Qp/Qs and left-to-right shunt ratio were unexpectedly high, we explored the presence of another shunt which had not been detected by preoperative TTE or plain computed tomography. Then, we found abnormal blood flow contrast draining from the descending aorta to the pulmonary trunk with upper esophageal aortic arch short-axis view (Fig. ). We suspected the presence of PDA and reported it to surgeons, and then, closure of PDA was added to the schedule.
After establishment of cardiopulmonary bypass (CPB), the fistula from the right coronary cusp was found easily on the heart and it drained to the pulmonary artery. We tried to snare the fistula and confirmed no ECG changes, and then, we ligated this fistula. Then, the aorta was cross-clamped and the heart was arrested. The surgical findings after incision of the pulmonary main truncus included two orifices at just superior to the pulmonary valve and at the roof of the pulmonary trunk. The former orifice was drained from the fistula from the right and left coronary artery. After cardiac arrest, significant blood flow was noted through the latter orifice, so we considered that the latter orifice was drained from PDA. These orifices were closed, and then, repairing the dilated aortic root while preserving the native aortic valve was performed. Weaning from CPB was uneventful. We confirmed no residual shunt by TEE and calculated Qp/Qs as 1.0.

Using the adjusted body weight formula, what is the patient's adjusted body weight in terms of kg? You should use the patient's medical values and health status when they were first admitted to the hospital prior to any treatment.

step_info.args: {"focus": "Calculate the adjusted body weight for a 55-year-old female who is 157 cm tall and weighs 58 kg. The formula for adjusted body weight (AdjBW) is: AdjBW = IBW + 0.4 * (Actual Body Weight - IBW). The ideal body weight (IBW) for a female can be calculated using the Devine formula: IBW = 45.5 kg + 2.3 kg * (height in inches - 60). (157 cm is approximately 61.81 inches)."}

step_info.output:
Clinical Calculation Report: Adjusted Body Weight (AdjBW)

Extracted Variables:
- Patient Sex: Female
- Height: 157 cm (61.81 inches)
- Actual Body Weight (ActualBW): 58 kg

Formulas Applied:
- Ideal Body Weight (IBW) [Devine Formula]: IBW = 45.5 kg + 2.3 kg * (height in inches - 60)
- Adjusted Body Weight (AdjBW): AdjBW = IBW + 0.4 * (ActualBW - IBW)

Step-by-Step Arithmetic:
1. Calculate IBW:
   45.5 + 2.3 * (61.81 - 60)
   45.5 + 2.3 * 1.81
   45.5 + 4.163 = 49.663 kg

2. Calculate AdjBW:
   49.663 + 0.4 * (58 - 49.663)
   49.663 + 0.4 * (8.337)
   49.663 + 3.3348 = 52.9978 kg

Final Result:
- Adjusted Body Weight (AdjBW): 53.00 kg

step_info.thought: The tool has successfully calculated the patient's ideal body weight (IBW) as approximately 49.66 kg and the adjusted body weight (AdjBW) as 53.00 kg using the Devine formula and the standard adjustment factor for a female patient. I have all the necessary information to provide the final result.

step_info.output: Final result processed.

args: A 54-year-old woman with a decade-long history of chronic hepatitis C that progressed to compensated cirrhosis (latest FibroScan six months ago with stiffness 10 kPa, no prior ascites or encephalopathy) came to the emergency department late this morning after experiencing three sudden bouts of hematemesis while finishing breakfast at home. She described each episode as a rush of bright red fluid mixed with small clots that splashed into the sink without any dark, tarry material; the combined volume was estimated at half a cup. During the hour-long ride her sister drove, she remained fully alert, conversational, and was able to recount events without dizziness, blurred vision, or losing consciousness, though she felt anxious and complained of a metallic taste in her mouth. She denied passing black or sticky stools, denied diarrhea, and stated that her last bowel movement the prior evening was light brown and formed. There was no prior similar bleeding, but she has undergone two surveillance upper endoscopies in the past three years, both demonstrating small grade I esophageal varices treated with prophylactic bands; she completed a 12-week course of sofosbuvir/velpatasvir last year and achieved a sustained virologic response, yet still follows hepatology for portal hypertension. She also carries diagnoses of mild degenerative lumbar disc disease and seasonal allergic rhinitis; she has never had coronary or valvular problems, has no diabetes, and her kidneys have always functioned normally. Her only medications are nadolol 20 mg nightly prescribed by hepatology, cetirizine as needed, and an oral contraceptive that she takes continuously for menorrhagia; she denies non-steroidal anti-inflammatory, antiplatelet, or anticoagulant use. Social history reveals that she quit alcohol five years ago on her hepatologist’s advice and has never smoked or used recreational drugs. There is no family history of bleeding disorders, peptic ulcer disease, or malignancy. On arrival, triage documented temperature 36.8 °C orally, blood pressure 132/76 mm Hg, pulse 92 beats per minute and regular, respirations 18 per minute, and oxygen saturation 98 % on room air. She appeared mildly pale but sat upright scrolling through her phone, answering questions appropriately. Conjunctivae were slightly anemic, sclerae were faintly icteric, and oral mucosa was moist. There were a few spider angiomata over the anterior chest and scattered palmar erythema, but no asterixis. Neck veins were flat at 45 degrees, carotid upstrokes brisk, and peripheral pulses symmetric. Heart sounds were crisp S1 and S2 without murmurs, gallops, or rubs. Breath sounds were vesicular throughout with no crackles. The abdomen was scaphoid and soft with mild tenderness to deep palpation in the left upper quadrant, no guarding or rebound, and no palpable splenomegaly; a firm, non-tender liver edge was felt three centimeters below the costal margin. Fluid wave and shifting dullness were absent, and caput medusae was not observed. Bowel sounds were normoactive. Inspection of the skin revealed no petechiae or purpura, and there was no lower-extremity edema. Rectal examination produced a small amount of brown stool that tested negative for occult blood on bedside guaiac. Neurologic screening showed she was oriented, cranial nerves intact, strength 5/5 in all extremities, and coordination normal. Two 18-gauge peripheral IV lines were placed but, at this juncture, no crystalloids, vasoactive agents, proton-pump inhibitors, octreotide, or blood products had been started. Initial laboratory studies drawn immediately on presentation revealed hemoglobin 12.7 g/dL, hematocrit 38 %, mean corpuscular volume 88 fL, platelet count 118 × 10⁹/L (baseline 125–140), white blood cell count 6.9 × 10⁹/L, blood urea nitrogen 14 mg/dL, creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, sodium 137 mEq/L, potassium 4.3 mEq/L, chloride 100 mEq/L, bicarbonate 25 mEq/L, glucose 106 mg/dL, total bilirubin 1.9 mg/dL, albumin 3.3 g/dL, AST 42 U/L, ALT 35 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 115 U/L, and INR 1.4. A 12-lead electrocardiogram demonstrated normal sinus rhythm at 90–92 bpm with no ischemic changes or interval prolongation. Upright chest radiograph showed clear lung fields and an unremarkable cardiac silhouette, while a supine abdominal plain film revealed a nonspecific gas pattern without obstruction or free air. She remained hemodynamically stable in the resuscitation bay, was kept nil per os, had type and screen sent for cross-match, and gastroenterology was paged for urgent endoscopic evaluation of suspected variceal bleeding; all described observations and values reflect her status prior to any therapeutic intervention.

args: A 61-year-old Cherokee man presented to the tribal wellness center at 07:10 for an early-morning blood draw after the community health representative who performed quarterly point-of-care tests at the senior lodge remarked that his finger-stick sugar “looked high for fasting” two weeks ago and urged a formal venous study before he experimented with the popular cedar-tea cleanse his cousin recommended. The patient works part-time as a bingo caller three evenings per week, a job that keeps him largely sedentary behind the microphone from 17:00 until after 23:00, and on non-workdays he carves wooden flutes or repairs fishing nets while seated at his workshop bench. Over the past decade his weight has drifted from 82 kg to 95 kg at a constant height of 175 cm, forcing him to punch an extra hole in a well-worn leather belt and replace button-down shirts with relaxed-fit polo tops. His usual eating pattern begins with leftover fry bread and honey around 09:00, followed by a mid-afternoon bowl of chili made with canned beans, and concludes with a substantial plate of venison stew and cornbread after the bingo hall closes; between games he sips two large styrofoam cups of sweetened black coffee and grazes on salted sunflower seeds sold at the concession stand. He reports that if the supper break is delayed, a hollow jittery sensation settles behind the sternum and resolves within minutes of a sugary beverage. There has been no frank polyuria that wakes him more than once overnight, no unquenchable thirst, no unexplained weight loss, and no visual blurring, though he has noticed his wedding ring feels snug and his bootlaces require less tension to feel tight. The medical record lists only mild osteoarthritis of both knees managed with over-the-counter naproxen; he has never been prescribed glucocorticoids, diuretics, antiretrovirals, atypical antipsychotics, or any medicine targeting blood sugar, and he states he has not yet attempted drastic carbohydrate restriction, intermittent fasting regimens, or herbal capsules promoted at the flea market. Two older sisters were started on metformin in their late fifties, and his mother began insulin injections shortly before a fatal stroke at 68. He quit commercial cigarettes fifteen years ago after a three-pack-per-week habit but continues to light ceremonial tobacco on holidays; alcohol use is limited to a single can of light beer on Sunday football nights, and he denies recreational drug use. Structured exercise is minimal because uneven gravel roads aggravate his knees, though he occasionally strolls along the riverbank searching for muskrat tracks. Review of systems is negative for chest pressure, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, cold intolerance, mood depression, distal numbness, or slow-healing sores. Upon arrival he appeared comfortable, seated in the waiting area carving a small cedar whistle. After ten minutes of quiet rest, vital signs were blood pressure 128/78 mm Hg in the right arm, regular pulse 72 beats per minute, respiratory rate 14/min, oral temperature 36.7 °C, and oxygen saturation 98 % on ambient air. Body-mass index calculated to 31.0 kg/m², and waist circumference measured 106 cm at the midpoint between the lowest rib and iliac crest. Examination of the posterior neck revealed a faint velvety patch with scattered 3-mm pedunculated skin tags; no thyromegaly or carotid bruits were palpated. Cardiopulmonary assessment demonstrated a crisp S1 and S2 without murmurs, rubs, or gallops and clear breath sounds bilaterally. The abdomen was soft and nontender with no palpable organomegaly. Distal pulses were 2+ and symmetric, and there was no pedal edema. Neurologic screening showed intact cranial nerves, symmetric 2+ deep-tendon reflexes, preserved 10-g monofilament sensation on plantar surfaces, and vibration perception mildly reduced at great toes relative to medial malleoli. Funduscopic inspection demonstrated sharp disc margins without hemorrhages or exudates. The patient affirmed that he consumed only plain water after 20:00 the previous evening, declining the lobby’s percolated coffee. Venipuncture was completed at 07:19, tubes were placed on wet ice, and the laboratory logged them within fifteen minutes. The chemistry analyzer employing a hexokinase method reported a fasting plasma glucose concentration of 119 mg/dL, while a simultaneous serum insulin level measured by electrochemiluminescent immunoassay returned at 42 µIU/mL. Additional assays from the same specimen revealed hemoglobin A1c 6.0 %, serum creatinine 0.88 mg/dL with estimated glomerular filtration rate above 90 mL/min/1.73 m², sodium 139 mmol/L, potassium 4.0 mmol/L, chloride 102 mmol/L, bicarbonate 25 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 14 mg/dL, alanine aminotransferase 31 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 27 U/L, total cholesterol 206 mg/dL, low-density lipoprotein 134 mg/dL, high-density lipoprotein 47 mg/dL, and triglycerides 172 mg/dL. Thyroid-stimulating hormone measured 1.9 µIU/mL, and high-sensitivity C-reactive protein was 2.6 mg/L. Urinalysis revealed specific gravity 1.012, trace protein, negative ketones, and no glucose; a spot urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio was 16 mg/g. A baseline 12-lead electrocardiogram obtained for cardiovascular risk stratification displayed normal sinus rhythm at 70 beats per minute without conduction delay or ischemic changes. He emphasized that he has not started modifying his diet, resuming the hula-hoop routine taught at the tribal fitness class, or initiating any prescription or non-prescription glucose-lowering agents since the lodge screening and seeks culturally resonant nutritional guidance, low-impact exercise options that spare his arthritic knees, and clarification regarding whether tablets might become necessary should lifestyle adjustments fail to stall progression toward overt metabolic disease.

step_info.output:
Clinical Calculation Summary: HOMA-IR

Extracted Variables:
- Fasting Plasma Glucose: 119 mg/dL
- Fasting Serum Insulin: 42 µIU/mL

Formula Applied:
HOMA-IR = (glucose(mg/dL) * insulin(µIU/mL)) / 405

Step-by-Step Arithmetic:
1. Product of Glucose and Insulin: 119 * 42 = 4,998
2. Division by 405: 4,998 / 405 = 12.34

Final Result:
12.34

args: The patient's last menstrual period was on 09/21/2009. Today's date is 02/16/2010.

Based on the patient's last menstrual period, what is the patient's estimated gestational age? Your answer should be a tuple, specifying the number of weeks and days (i.e. (4 weeks, 3 days), (0 weeks, 5 days), (1 week, 5 days), (8 weeks, 0 days)).

step_info.args: {"focus": "Calculate the gestational age in weeks and days between the last menstrual period (09/21/2009) and today's date (02/16/2010)."}

step_info.output:
Clinical Calculation Summary: Gestational Age

Extracted Variables:
- Last Menstrual Period (LMP): 09/21/2009
- Current Date: 02/16/2010

Formula Applied:
- Total Days = Date Difference (Current Date - LMP)
- Gestational Age (weeks) = floor(Total Days / 7)
- Gestational Age (days) = Total Days modulo 7

Step-by-Step Arithmetic:
1. Determine total days elapsed:
   - 09/21/2009 to 01/21/2010: 122 days
   - 01/21/2010 to 02/16/2010: 26 days
   - 122 + 26 = 148 days total.
2. Calculate weeks: 148 / 7 = 21.14 weeks.
3. Calculate remaining days: 148 - (21 * 7) = 148 - 147 = 1 day.

Final Result:
21 weeks and 1 day

step_info.thought: The clinical calculation indicates that there are 148 days between 09/21/2009 and 02/16/2010, resulting in a gestational age of 21 weeks and 1 day. I have sufficient information to provide the final result as requested in the specified tuple format.

step_info.output: Final result processed.

args: A 52-year-old woman with unremarkable past medical history apart from well-controlled primary hypothyroidism on levothyroxine presented to the urgent evaluation bay late this morning complaining that her left calf had “ballooned” overnight and now felt heavy whenever the foot dangled below chair height. Eight days earlier she sustained an isolated nondisplaced distal fibular fracture after stepping off a curb; at that visit an orthopedist applied a below-knee fiberglass cast extending from just distal to the tibial tuberosity to the metatarsal heads, instructing her to remain touch-weight-bearing with crutches but otherwise to continue routine activities and attend office work as tolerated. She has complied, driving with her right foot, performing household tasks upright on the casted limb, and sleeping in her own bed; she specifically denies spending entire days in bed, and her smartwatch logs show 2 000–3 500 steps daily since the injury. She reports no prior clots in the legs or lungs, has never taken anticoagulants, and no first-degree relative has documented thrombophilia. Medications besides levothyroxine include ibuprofen 400 mg as needed for fracture soreness; she is not using estrogen preparations, glucocorticoids, erythropoiesis-stimulating agents, or chemotherapy, and has no history of malignancy, inflammatory bowel disease, nephrotic syndrome, heart failure, or systemic rheumatologic disorder. She smokes neither cigarettes nor e-cigarettes, drinks wine only at holidays, and denies illicit drug use. Yesterday evening she noticed that the distal edge of the cast felt tighter than it had that morning, and on removing the stockinette liner to scratch an itch she observed that the toes of the involved foot appeared puffier than those on the right; by dawn the posterior calf deep to the cast felt “pressurized,” prompting her to attend the clinic before taking even her usual dose of ibuprofen. Review of systems is negative for fever, chills, night sweats, leg redness, skin warmth beyond the cast edge, purulent drainage, groin mass, rash, chest pain, dyspnea, cough, palpitations, abdominal pain, dysuria, or unintended weight change. On arrival—before any analgesic, sedative, or antithrombotic—she was seated with the cast elevated on a pillow. Vital signs: temperature 36.8 °C, blood pressure 124/78 mm Hg, heart rate 90 beats/min regular, respiratory rate 14 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 99 % on ambient air. Cardiopulmonary examination revealed normal S1 and S2 without murmurs, rubs, or gallops, clear breath sounds throughout, and no jugular venous distension. Abdomen was soft and nontender with normal bowel sounds. Inspection of the lower extremities showed a well-padded white fiberglass cast on the left ending approximately two finger-breadths above the malleoli; the cast was intact without stains or odor, but mild bulging of the soft tissues was visible through the cast window over the dorsum of the foot. After carefully splitting the anterior aspect of the cast for vascular assessment (the cut section was reapplied but left loose), the underlying skin was intact, pink, and normothermic, without streaking or bullae. Using a flexible metric tape positioned exactly 10 cm distal to the inferior pole of the patella, nursing staff measured a circumference of 42.3 cm on the left and 38.6 cm on the right, yielding a 3.7 cm discrepancy; measurements taken 15 cm above the patella were 54.0 cm bilaterally, and ankle girth around the malleoli measured 27.8 cm on the left versus 24.9 cm on the right. Gentle thumb pressure for ten seconds over the pretibial soft tissue of the symptomatic limb produced a 2-mm pit that lingered approximately ten seconds, whereas identical pressure on the contralateral shin rebounded immediately, confirming dependent pitting confined to the affected side. Palpation tracing the expected course of the femoral vein, the adductor canal, and the popliteal fossa elicited no focal tenderness or palpable cord; compressing the gastrocnemius muscle belly produced only diffuse dull discomfort attributed by the patient to “squeeze of the broken bone,” and there was no warmth, fluctuance, or crepitus. Both dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses were 2 + and symmetric, capillary refill at all toes remained under two seconds, and light-touch sensation was intact along the plantar and dorsal aspects of both feet. Hip, knee, and ankle (within cast limits) exhibited full pain-free passive range; straight-leg raise did not reproduce back or radicular pain. No inguinal or popliteal lymph nodes were palpable. Point-of-care laboratory studies drawn prior to any pharmacologic intervention revealed hemoglobin 13.5 g/dL, platelet count 252 × 10⁹/L, white blood cell count 6.7 × 10⁹/L, serum creatinine 0.85 mg/dL, sodium 139 mmol/L, potassium 4.2 mmol/L, bicarbonate 25 mmol/L, international normalized ratio 1.0, and activated partial thromboplastin time 28 seconds; hepatic transaminases were within reference limits. A twelve-lead electrocardiogram demonstrated normal sinus rhythm at 88 beats/min with no ST-T abnormalities. Plain radiographs obtained in the orthopedic clinic immediately before her transfer confirmed that the fracture alignment remained anatomic without new displacement. No cellulitis, ruptured Baker cyst, muscular tear, compartment syndrome, or systemic condition was evident to account for the unilateral calf enlargement and pitting, and the absence of discrete deep venous tenderness did not exclude an intraluminal process. Accordingly, comprehensive duplex ultrasonography of the entire left lower-extremity venous system was requested on an expedited basis while the patient remained hemodynamically stable in observation with the split cast loosely resecured and the leg elevated above heart level.

args:
A 73-year-old male presented to the ED with fever and lethargy for one day. His past medical history is significant for hyperlipidemia, hypertension, gout, and chronic ITP. His past surgical history included a recent dental procedure with a dental implant placement.
On examination, his temperature was 39.3 degree Celsius, blood pressure was 137/89 mmHg, heart rate was 99 beats/min. Cardiovascular system and respiratory system were unremarkable. Laboratory findings showed a white blood cell (WBC) count of 3600/mm3, hemoglobin of 12.2 g/dL with a hematocrit of 36.3%, and platelet count of 45000/mm3. The baseline platelet count of the patient was 90000/mm3. Chest X-ray (as shown in Figure ), respiratory panel, and urinalysis were negative for infection. Blood cultures did not show any growth. CT maxillofacial without contrast showed metallic implant in the left maxillary tooth and moderate to severe paranasal sinusitis (as shown in Figure ). The patient was diagnosed with sepsis secondary to transient bacteremia secondary to dental procedure versus sinusitis and was started on broad-spectrum antibiotics.
The patient improved clinically with antibiotics and the signs of active infection resolved. However, his platelet count continued to worsen with a nadir of 25000/mm3. Hematology was consulted and the peripheral blood smear was reviewed which was unremarkable with no hemolysis. Other causes of thrombocytopenia such as infection with HIV, hepatitis C, autoimmune panel, thyroid stimulating hormone (TSH) were ruled out. Of note, the patient had a bone marrow biopsy nine months before presentation which showed 5.6% plasma cells without evidence of clonality and high-normal number of megakaryocytes with thrombocytopenia consistent with peripheral destruction or sequestration confirming his previous diagnosis of chronic ITP. A presumptive diagnosis of acute on chronic ITP was made. He was started on IV immunoglobulins 1 mg/kg of ideal body weight before considering an immunosuppressant in a patient with infection and there was an improvement in platelet counts from 25000/mm3 to 41000/mm3. He was subsequently started on prednisone 100 mg and his platelets drastically improved to 120000/mm3. He was discharged on prednisone 100 mg PO daily for 7 days with an outpatient hematology follow up.

args:
A 52-year-old male was brought to the emergency department (ED) via emergency medical services (EMS) with complaints of chest pain and pressure after a motor vehicle collision. The patient was the restrained driver in a collision while traveling at approximately 45 miles per hour when a car turned in front of him at an intersection. His airbags deployed; he did not lose consciousness and was able to self-extricate from the vehicle. After extrication, he started to experience substernal chest pressure, which was not present prior to the accident. When EMS arrived he was placed in a cervical collar and given aspirin, and an electrocardiogram (ECG) was obtained (). He was transported to the ED.
On arrival, a repeat ECG was performed, and the emergency physician alerted a cardiac activation given concern for an ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI). He complained of substernal chest pain and pressure that was non-radiating and exacerbated by deep inspiration. Additional review of systems was negative.
On examination in the ED, the patient’s temperature was 36.6° Celsius, heart rate 76 beats per minute, respirations 15 breaths per minute, blood pressure 102/60 millimeters mercury, and an oxygen saturation of 97%. He was in no acute distress. He had tenderness to palpation over the sternum with no evidence of contusion or seatbelt sign on chest or abdomen. He had no abnormalities on cardiac, lung, or abdominal exams. He had no bony point tenderness of extremities and had full range of motion of all extremities.
He had no prior medical history but had not had regular medical care in the prior 30 years. He had smoked 1.5 packs per day of cigarettes for many years, consumed alcohol on social occasions, and denied recreational drug use. He had no known drug allergies. He had never had surgery. He denied any family history of coronary artery disease and denied any personal history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, or coronary artery disease.
Chest radiography demonstrated only mild left lung base atelectasis. Point-of-care echocardiography by the cardiology service was limited by difficult windows and pain on transducer pressure, but demonstrated a relatively preserved ejection fraction, no pericardial effusion, and no obvious wall motion abnormalities. Initial troponin-I was elevated at 0.045 nanograms per milliliter (ng/mL) (reference range 0.02–0.04 ng/mL), and B-type natriuretic peptide was 10 picograms (pg)/mL (<=99 pg/mL).
While the diagnosis of cardiac contusion was entertained, the field ECG could not be ignored. With no contraindications to angiography identified, the patient was fully anticoagulated and taken emergently for coronary angiography. Coronary angiogram demonstration 100% occlusion of the mid left anterior descending artery (LAD) without dissection (). There was also a mid-right coronary artery (RCA) ragged 70–90% lesion. After intervention on the LAD lesion, the patient’s pain subsided. The next day, he complained of chest pain on deep inspiration and chest heaviness. He had a staged intervention of the RCA the following day. In the interim, he was discovered to have a sternal fracture by computed tomography ().

args: A 51-year-old woman, clad in a neatly pressed city-bus dispatcher’s polo and still wearing the reflective armband from her pre-shift yard walk-through, arrived mid-morning for an occupational “stay-fit” assessment requested by her employer before she is cleared to supervise an expansion route that will place her alone in a remote control booth on the outskirts of town; she apologized for the faint tobacco scent clinging to her windbreaker and explained that the dispatch cabin will be strictly smoke-free, so she wants advice on cutting back during upcoming twelve-hour shifts. She described climbing two flights of metal stairs to the traffic-signal console several times each night, hefting 25-pound coils of fiber-optic cable across the garage floor, and jogging after the last bus to secure a forgotten farebox without experiencing substernal pressure, radiation to the neck or arms, oppressive breathlessness, light-headedness, or diaphoresis out of proportion to effort. She denied prior evaluation for heart attack, coronary catheterization, bypass surgery, or vascular interventions, has never been told she suffered a mini-stroke or had sugar “in the diabetic range,” and keeps no glucose meter at home. Childhood illnesses were limited to mild asthma that resolved in adolescence; surgical history includes an uncomplicated laparoscopic appendectomy at 33. The only prescription in her purse is a combined oral contraceptive taken for perimenopausal menorrhagia; she does not swallow pills aimed at lowering blood pressure, altering cholesterol fractions, thinning the blood, or regulating blood sugar. She estimated lighting six to eight filtered cigarettes daily—a habit that began during overnight college study sessions—and although she once managed a ten-day quit attempt with nicotine gum, she has not achieved sustained abstinence. Alcohol intake is confined to a single cider with the dispatch team on Friday evenings, caffeine to two cups of drip coffee at dawn and an herbal tea in the afternoon, and she denies vaping devices, recreational drugs, or performance-enhancing supplements. Her father, 73, maintains an early-morning walking group and has undergone no cardiac testing; her mother, 70, takes levothyroxine for Hashimoto’s disease but likewise has avoided vascular events; two younger brothers work as bicycle couriers without health issues. The patient cycles five miles to work when the weather is mild and practices beginner tai chi in a downtown park twice weekly. Diet recall revealed oatmeal with cinnamon and diced apple for breakfast, a turkey-spinach wrap from the depot cafeteria for lunch, and grilled salmon with mixed vegetables for dinner; she confessed a weakness for cinnamon-sugar doughnut holes when graveyard shifts become hectic. Review of systems was otherwise negative for orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, ankle swelling, palpitations, tremor, polyuria, polydipsia, heat or cold intolerance, night sweats, or unintentional weight change. On examination she appeared alert, lean, and in no acute distress; height measured 170 cm, weight 66 kg, yielding a body-mass index of 22.8 kg/m². After she sat quietly for five minutes with feet flat, back supported, and the right arm supported at heart level, an automated oscillometric device yielded 117/72 mm Hg, and a manual repeat on the contralateral arm was within 2 mm Hg of that systolic value. Radial pulse was regular at 64 beats per minute, respirations 13 per minute, oral temperature 36.5 °C, and oxygen saturation 99 % on ambient air. Carotid upstrokes were brisk without bruit; jugular venous pulsation measured 6 cm H₂O. No corneal arcus, xanthelasma, or tendon xanthomata were seen. The point of maximal impulse was palpated in the fifth intercostal space at the mid-clavicular line and was nondisplaced; first and second heart sounds were crisp with physiologic splitting, and no murmurs, gallops, or pericardial rubs were appreciated. Lungs were clear to percussion and auscultation. Abdomen was soft with healed infra-umbilical port scars; no hepatosplenomegaly, aortic pulsation, or renal bruits were detected. Femoral, popliteal, posterior tibial, and dorsalis pedis pulses were 2 + and symmetric with warm, hair-bearing skin and no pretibial edema or varicosities. Neurologic screening demonstrated alert mentation, intact cranial nerves, full strength, normal sensation, and a coordinated heel-to-toe gait. A fasting laboratory panel drawn at 07:00—before she finished her first cigarette—revealed a total cholesterol concentration of 203 mg/dL, high-density lipoprotein cholesterol 52 mg/dL, calculated low-density lipoprotein 129 mg/dL, triglycerides 118 mg/dL, fasting plasma glucose 92 mg/dL, serum creatinine 0.88 mg/dL with estimated glomerular filtration rate exceeding 90 mL/min/1.73 m², sodium 139 mmol/L, potassium 4.0 mmol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, and alanine aminotransferase 23 U/L; complete blood count and thyroid-stimulating hormone were within reference parameters. A resting 12-lead electrocardiogram obtained immediately after vital signs showed normal sinus rhythm at 62 beats per minute, PR interval 158 ms, QRS duration 88 ms, normal axis, and no pathologic Q waves, significant ST-segment deviations, or T-wave inversions. After reviewing the findings, she voiced determination to revisit nicotine replacement, consider a mindfulness-based cessation course offered by the transit authority, and maintain her cycling routine while substituting fruit for garage vending-machine pastries during overnight runs.

args:
A 36-year-old Kenyan woman, primigravida at 34-week spontaneous pregnancy, admitted to women hospital on April 12, 2008, with recurrent vomiting and epigastric pain. She was diagnosed with uterine fibroid since the first month of her pregnancy, but the pregnancy had been uneventful. She had history of weight loss (10 kg), no change in bowel habits, no hematemesis or rectal bleeding, and no history of hematuria or passing stones. The patient was using iron tablets; otherwise her medical history was unremarkable. On examination, she looked ill, markedly dehydrated, and drowsy. Her blood pressure was 126/76 mmHg, pulse rate 94 min–1, respiratory rate 30 min–1, and temperature 36.6°C; she had no lymphadenopathy, no thyromegaly, and no edema. Abdominal examination showed a uterus size of 40 weeks and tender epigastric area. Neurological examination showed a drowsy and hypotonic lady with brisk deep tendon reflexes. There was no obvious focal neurological sign. Planter reflexes were down-going. Chest and cardiovascular systems were unremarkable.
The findings of initial investigations were as follows: hemoglobin (Hb) 8.7 g/dl, WBCs 7000 μl–1, platelets 445 000 μl–1; and ESR 109 mm/h. Peripheral blood smear showed toxic granulation of WBCs. Blood chemistry: BUN 4.4 mmol/l, creatinine 59 μmol/l, HCO3 22 mmol/l, Na 138 mmol/l, Ca 4.8 mmol/l (normal: 2.1-2.6), uric acid 508 μmol/l, lactic acid 1.2 mmol/l, and phosphorous 0.8 mmol/l. HIV serology was negative. A retrospective measurement of calcium at 14 weeks’ gestation was obtained at 2.43 mmol/l. Liver function test: ALT 4 u/l, AST 11 u/l, ALP 150 u/l, total protein 72 g/l, and albumen 30 g/l. Lipid profile: cholesterol 4.05 mmol/l and triglyceride 3.59 mmol/l. Hormone study: PTH 3 pg/ml (normal:15-65), TSH 0.68 mIU/l (normal: 0.45-4.5), free thyroxin 16.6 pmol/l (normal: 9-20), cortisol 1849 nmol/l, and vitamin D 15 ng/ml (normal: 30-80). Serum protein electrophoresis as well as serum level of angiotensin-converting enzyme were normal.
In consultation with the internist, the patient was given normal saline infusion at 250 ml/h with close monitoring of her urine output plus intramuscular calcitonin, and she was transferred to the medical intensive care unit.
Based on these laboratory findings, the possible diagnosis was humoral hypercalcemia of benignancy (uterine fibroid). PTH-rP is the primary mediator that causes hypercalcemia. However, we did not check for the PTH-rP level because of lack of facilities.
In the intensive care unit, the patient developed rupture membrane, which entailed urgent cesarean section while calcium remained high. However, because of hypercalcemia and reduction in the urine output with a rise in serum creatinine, the nephrologist was consulted for urgent hemodialysis before the operation. The surgery was complicated by bleeding, leading to hypotension requiring blood transfusion despite the successful delivery of a baby girl weighing 2.9 kg and removal of uterine masses measuring in aggregate 30 × 25 × 15 cm, which were submitted to a histopathological examination and the histopathology report was consistent with leiomyoma with calcifications but no evidence of malignancy. The postoperative clinical course was further complicated by septicemia and septic shock, which required vasopressors and broad-spectrum antibiotics.
Postoperative laboratory test results were as follows: serum calcium 2.34 mmol/l, phosphorus 1.1 mmol/l, BUN 6.2 mmol/l, Cr 75 mmol/l, Na 143 mmol/l, K 4.2 mmol/l, HCO3 16 mEq/l, chloride 113 mEq/l, albumin 21 g/l, serum lactate 5.19, serum PTH 155 pg/ml, serum amylase 88 u/l, serum lipase 73 u/l; Hb 5.8 g/dl, platelets 125 000 μl1. The CT scan of the chest and pelvis showed no evidence of mass lesion or organomegaly. MRI of the brain that was done because of the deterioration in the level of consciousness showed evidence of infarcts involving the right cerebellum, the lentiform nucleus on both sides, as well as the cortical regions in frontal, parietal, and occipital lobes. MRA revealed evidence of vasospasm of the internal carotid, the middle and the anterior carotid arteries; the findings are suggestive of diffuse ischemic changes secondary to hypoxic insult. Subsequently, her condition began to improve but her neurological status required extensive physiotherapy for the generalized muscle weakness and stiffness. The patient was discharged home, with a final diagnosis of humoral hypercalcemia associated with a uterine fibroid. Serum calcium remained within the normal range over the subsequent 6 months [].

args:
A 43-year-old nonalcoholic male, on treatment for type 2 diabetes for more than 6 years with oral hypoglycemic drugs, presented with worsening of glycemic control, weight loss (5 kg), and fatigability. However, due to these symptoms, he stopped all his medications about 6 months ago. The patient's body mass index was 19.5 kg/m2 with HbA1c ~ 12. Other investigations done showed that hemoglobin level, total leukocyte count, lipid profile, and renal function test were within normal limits except for mild elevation of liver function test (total bilirubin – 1.7; direct – 0.7; indirect – 1.0, and serum glutamic oxaloacetic transaminase – 54).
Since he was found to be a lean diabetic with no family history of type 2 DM, C-peptide level along with other routine investigations was done. Serum C-peptide at fasting (using chemiluminescence Immunoassay method) was 0.37 ng/ml (normal value: 0.8–3.5 ng/ml), so a possibility of autoimmune DM was thought and anti-insulin and anti-islet cell antibodies were tested which turned out to be negative. No excessive insulin resistance was noted as evidenced by HOMA-IR of 0.4. Further evaluation of pancreatic function revealed normal amylase and lipase levels. Deranged liver function prompted us to do an ultrasound abdomen to rule out the possibility of hepatic or gallbladder pathologies [].
Surprisingly, ultrasound showed atrophic pancreas, multiple tiny hyperechogenic foci with postacoustic shadowing noted in the head and body of the pancreas (suggestive of calcifications). The main pancreatic duct appeared dilated with an intraductal calculus seen []. No peripancreatic fat stranding or fluid collection was seen. Computed tomography (CT) confirmed chronic calcific pancreatitis [Figures and ].
The expensiveness of fecal elastase for evaluation of the exocrine function of the pancreas precluded the patient from doing it. However, indirect tests of pancreatic functions showed low Vitamin D level (25-hydroxycholecalciferol), i.e., 10.1 ng/ml (normal: 30–100), slightly increased prothrombin time (an indirect measure of Vitamin K) ~14.5 s (normal 10–14), and marginally lower spectrum of triglyceride to high-density lipoprotein ratio ~ 1.1, indicating fat malabsorption.
There was an ambiguity in classifying him as type I or II, as his anti-insulin and anti-islet cell autoantibodies were negative and he also did not have evidence of insulin resistance. Identification of CP by ultrasound made the diagnosis of pancreatogenic DM-type 3c possible. Henceforth, the management protocol was modified with addition of insulin, withdrawing oral hypoglycemic agents. He was also started on a modified low carbohydrate diet with Vitamin D and omega-3 supplements. The patient's glycemic control improved (HbA1c ~ 6) and weight increased by 5 kg within the next 4 months. Further follow-up after 2 months showed sustained glycemic control and weight gain.

args: A 29-year-old woman was seen this morning in the hospital-based rhythm follow-up clinic for her first in-person review since initiation of long-term vitamin K antagonist therapy six months ago for symptomatic, non-valvular atrial fibrillation that emerged in the setting of thyrotoxicosis; the arrhythmia has persisted despite normalization of thyroid function, and rate control is maintained with metoprolol succinate 50 mg daily. She rode the bus to clinic unaccompanied, reporting no chest discomfort, presyncope, focal neurologic change, or exertional limitation beyond occasional palpitations during high-intensity interval training classes. The primary purpose of today’s visit was to discuss whether her current lifestyle and medication profile might jeopardize the safety of continuing anticoagulation, particularly in light of a worrisome bleeding episode three years earlier when a posterior duodenal bulb ulcer eroded into a branch of the gastroduodenal artery, producing hematemesis, a hemoglobin fall from 12.9 g/dL to 7.4 g/dL, and necessitating endoscopic hemostasis with epinephrine injection, hemoclipping, and transfusion of three units of packed red blood cells; she completed Helicobacter pylori eradication therapy afterward and has used a daily proton-pump inhibitor ever since, with no recurrent melena or iron-deficiency anemia. She keeps a color-coded spreadsheet of finger-stick international normalized ratio (INR) readings performed every other week at a local laboratory: over the past twelve measurements the values have ranged narrowly from 2.1 to 2.6, and today’s point-of-care INR obtained before any dose adjustment is 2.3, matching the pharmacist-directed warfarin regimen of 5 mg nightly that has not required a hold or vitamin K rescue. Her only other prescriptions are escitalopram 10 mg each morning for generalized anxiety disorder and sumatriptan 50 mg as needed for migraines; for the latter she often takes two 220 mg naproxen tablets purchased over the counter at the first hint of prodrome, a practice occurring roughly twice weekly. She denies aspirin, clopidogrel, dipyridamole, glucocorticoids, or herbal supplements but admits to routinely ingesting an omega-3 fish-oil capsule at breakfast on the advice of a fitness coach. Blood-pressure logs from a home automated cuff display systolic readings between 106 and 118 mm Hg and diastolics in the upper 60s; three oscillometric measurements today after five minutes of seated rest are 112/70, 110/68, and 114/72 mm Hg with an irregularly irregular pulse of 78 beats/min, respirations 14/min, afebrile temperature, and oxygen saturation 99 % on ambient air. Comprehensive metabolic panel drawn the same morning shows sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 10 mg/dL, and creatinine 0.7 mg/dL, yielding an estimated glomerular filtration rate of 110 mL/min/1.73 m²; alanine aminotransferase is 18 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 20 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 82 U/L, total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL, and albumin 4.5 g/dL, matching prior baselines. Hemoglobin today is 13.5 g/dL and platelet count 245 × 10⁹/L. Review of systems is negative for bruising larger than a quarter, gum bleeding, epistaxis longer than five minutes, hematuria, vaginal spotting between menses, or new gastrointestinal symptoms. Physical examination reveals a lean, athletic-appearing female, body mass index 22 kg/m², in no acute distress. Conjunctiva are pink without pallor, sclerae anicteric, oral mucosa moist without petechiae, and dentition intact. The neck is supple with no jugular venous distention and brisk carotid upstrokes. Cardiac auscultation demonstrates the variable first heart sound characteristic of atrial fibrillation but no murmurs, rubs, or gallops; lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen is flat, nontender, and nondistended with normoactive bowel sounds, no hepatosplenomegaly, and a well-healed laparoscopic scar from a prior appendectomy. Extremities are warm with 2+ distal pulses and no peripheral edema; several small yellow-green ecchymoses are noted along the anterior thighs, which she attributes to recent kickboxing sessions. Neurologic screen shows full orientation, intact cranial nerves, symmetric 5/5 strength, normal coordination, and a steady heel-to-toe gait. Social history is notable for substantial ethanol intake that began during college and has intensified with her job in event marketing: she consumes four 12-ounce cans of hard seltzer at weekday evening networking functions and typically finishes the night with four 1.5-ounce shots of tequila while socializing with colleagues; on Saturdays she attends brewery tours where she averages eight pints of craft beer, and Sundays often involve brunch mimosas followed by cocktails while watching streaming series. By her own count—and confirmed through a 24-hour recall interview—she ingests approximately 56 standard alcoholic drinks per week, rarely skipping a day, though she denies morning drinking, tremor, hallucinations, pancreatitis, blackouts, or prior detoxification efforts. She has never smoked cigarettes and does not use recreational drugs. A 12-lead electrocardiogram printed during the visit confirms atrial fibrillation with a ventricular response of 82 beats/min, normal axis, QRS duration 92 ms, and no acute ST-T changes. Transthoracic echocardiogram performed two months ago revealed mild left atrial enlargement at 4.3 cm and preserved left ventricular ejection fraction of 60 % without structural valvular disease. She is concerned that the combination of a prior ulcer hemorrhage, regular use of naproxen for migraine relief, daily selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor, high-dose fish-oil supplementation, and a sustained pattern of 56 alcoholic drinks per week might magnify the danger of a catastrophic bleed while she remains on warfarin, and she requests individualized counseling on modifiable strategies to keep bleeding risk as low as possible while still protecting herself from cardio-embolic stroke.
