args: A 58-year-old woman with a fifteen-year history of chronic hepatitis B infection complicated by compensated cirrhosis was referred to the liver clinic for her semi-annual surveillance visit after recent primary care laboratory work disclosed a slight rise in her coagulation profile. She denied abdominal bloating, ankle swelling, early satiety, breathlessness when lying flat, gastrointestinal bleeding, changes in bowel habits, confusion, daytime sleepiness, tremor, or difficulty performing her job as an accountant. Her past medical history included well-controlled hypertension managed with amlodipine 5 mg daily, class II obesity treated through a structured nutrition program, and osteopenia for which she took weekly alendronate; she had never consumed alcohol, had no history of recreational drug use, and completed a course of tenofovir five years earlier that rendered her viral load undetectable, with maintenance entecavir 0.5 mg daily ever since. Family history was significant for a mother with breast cancer and a father with type 2 diabetes; no relatives were known to have liver disease. Review of systems was otherwise unremarkable. Immunizations were current, including hepatitis A vaccination, and she recently underwent screening colonoscopy that was normal. Vital signs at presentation revealed temperature 36.8 °C, blood pressure 128/68 mm Hg, heart rate 78 beats/min, respiratory rate 13 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 99 % on ambient air. She appeared well nourished, alert, and conversant, volunteered a detailed medication list without hesitation, and easily performed serial sevens and recited the current news headlines, suggesting intact higher cortical function. Conjunctivae were clear without scleral yellowing, parotid glands were normal in size, and the oral cavity was moist with no fetor. There were a few spider angiomas over the upper sternum, but no palmar erythema, digital clubbing, or muscle wasting. Cardiopulmonary examination demonstrated a regular rhythm without murmurs and clear breath sounds bilaterally. Her abdomen was flat and soft with a well-healed Pfannenstiel scar from a remote hysterectomy; percussion produced resonant tones across the flanks with no shift when she rolled laterally, and an elicited fluid wave was absent even when counterpressure was applied. The liver edge was palpable 1 cm below the right costal margin, firm yet smooth, non-tender, and the spleen was not felt. There were no abdominal wall collaterals, no umbilical protrusion, and no pedal edema. Neurologic examination showed symmetric strength, intact sensation, rapid alternating movements without hesitation, and a steady narrow-based gait. Laboratory studies drawn that morning prior to any therapy demonstrated total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, serum albumin 4.5 g/dL, alanine aminotransferase 44 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 48 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 118 U/L, gamma-glutamyl transferase 151 U/L, sodium 139 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 14 mg/dL, creatinine 0.78 mg/dL, glucose 112 mg/dL, hemoglobin 13.3 g/dL, white blood cell count 5.9 ×10⁹/L, platelet count 142 ×10⁹/L, and a prothrombin time corresponding to an international normalized ratio of 1.45. Alfa-fetoprotein measured concurrently was 5 ng/mL. Hepatitis B surface antigen remained positive whereas e-antigen was negative, and viral DNA by PCR was below the lower limit of quantification. A focused point-of-care ultrasound obtained during the visit displayed a nodular hepatic contour with coarse echotexture, patent portal and hepatic veins with hepatopetal flow, no intrahepatic mass, spleen length of 13 cm, and no detectable free intraperitoneal fluid. Electrocardiogram recorded normal sinus rhythm at 76 beats/min without QT prolongation, and a screening chest radiograph revealed clear lung fields. Having reviewed the data, the hepatology team emphasized continued antiviral adherence, avoidance of non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, maintenance of dietary sodium restriction to less than 2 g daily, and routine imaging every six months for hepatocellular carcinoma surveillance.

A-61-year-old male of Han ancestry was admitted to our hospital on July 15, 2020 due to a 4-yr history of progressive swollen soft masses at multiple sites.
The patient presented with progressive soft masses over the abdomen for more than four years, swellings in the mandible, back of the neck and elbows also gradually enlarged during these four years.
The patient was a heavy drinker with an average of 500 mL liquor per day for 45 years. He had a smoking history of approximately 20 cigarettes/day for 45 years,. The patient was diagnosed with type 2 diabetes more than 20 years previously, with poorly controlled glucose for many years. He had a history of hypertension for 18 years and did not take any antihypertensive drugs.
He had no family history of similar diseases and denied previous special drug use.
Physical examination upon admission revealed the following: The patient was conscious; a body mass index of 21.0 kg/m2; subcutaneous masses in the mandible, back of the neck, abdomen and elbows (Figure ); no signs of moon face, paper skin, hirsutism, or purple stria.
Laboratory tests showed a total cholesterol level of 92.16 mg/dL, low-density lipoprotein cholesterol level of 35.28 mg/dL, high-density lipoprotein cholesterol level of 37.44 mg/dL, triglyceride level of 20.7 mg/dL, hemoglobin A1c of 5.4% and 24 h urinary albumin was 343.5 mg/d. Thyroid function was in the normal range. His basal cortisol level as well as his 1 mg dexamethasone suppression test were normal. Hepatitis B, hepatitis C, human immunodeficiency virus and syphilis were all negative.
Ultrasonography showed diffuse lipomatosis and lymph node echo in the neck in January 2018. The thickest part of the mass in the neck was about 28 mm. In July 2020, ultrasonography showed that the thickest part of the mass in the neck was about 44 mm. Ultrasonography of the elbow suggested that there was a hypoechoic mass between the palmaris muscles on both sides of the elbow, with a range of about 51 mm × 15 mm × 34 mm on the left and 46 mm × 14 mm × 35 mm on the right. The boundary was clear and the internal echo was not uniform, with strong echoes of several fiber spacers, similar to the echoes of adipose tissue. Simultaneously, excessive fat deposition was suggested on the mandible and the abdominal area of the patient by ultrasonic examination. Computed tomography showed diffuse and marked fatty deposits in the subcutaneous superficial fascia space of the neck without internal lesions (Figure ). A breast ultrasound examination showed that both sides of the breast had excessive fat deposition and a flaky strong echo was seen in the deep side of the areola. The left side was about 33 mm × 3 mm × 25 mm and the right side was about 44 mm × 8 mm × 31 mm. The internal lobular structure was disordered, and small hypoechoic areas were seen, but no obvious mass echo and mammary duct expansion was found.

calculator_name: CKD-EPI Equations for Glomerular Filtration Rate

args:
A 49-year-old Dutch male patient came to King Chulalongkorn Memorial Hospital (KCMH) with a complaint of low-grade fever and profuse sweating at night for 1 week. Three weeks earlier, he was diagnosed HIV infection when antiretroviral medications comprising of tenofovir, emtricitabine, and efavirenz were prescribed. He denied taking any over-the-counter drugs. The physical examination was unremarkable except for a body temperature of 38°C. He also had normotension (blood pressure 125/75 mmHg) without orthostatic hypotension or other signs of volume depletion. The chest X-ray showed miliary pulmonary nodules compatible with miliary tuberculosis which was later confirmed by positive polymerase-chain-reaction for Mycobacterium tuberculosis in his sputum. Disseminated tuberculosis was promptly diagnosed, and antituberculosis treatment (isoniazid, rifampicin, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol) was planned. However, he also had severe azotemia at admission (BUN 53.6 mmol/L, Cr 1,230 µmol/L) in contrast to his baseline values from one month earlier (Cr 115 µmol/L). At that time, there were no evidences of uremic symptoms or volume overload, and he still voided 500 mL of urine per day. Urinalysis revealed isosthenuria with bland urinary sediments (specific gravity 1.010, pH 5.0, albuminuria trace, glycose negative, WBC 0-1/hpf, and RBC 0-1/hpf). Renal ultrasonography demonstrated normal size and contour of both kidneys. Urine biomarker for renal tubular injury, neutrophil gelatinase-associated lipocalin (NGAL), was markedly elevated (7,891 ng/mL). Acute kidney injury was diagnosed and likely caused by nephrotoxic acute tubular necrosis (ATN) even though a renal biopsy had not been done. In the absence of other offending drugs or conditions, tenofovir was suspected to be a causal drug for ATN resulting in an adjustment of the antiretroviral regimen (abacavir, lamivudine, and raltegravir).
In the absence of uremic symptom or volume overload, PD was, nevertheless, initiated due to high level of nitrogen catabolites. The flexible double-cuffed PD catheter was inserted on day 4 of admission, and automated PD (Homechoice cycler®; Baxter) using a total dialysate (Dianeal®; Baxter) volume of 10 L (initial fill volume of 700 mL, 14 cycles, 20 hours) was promptly started on the same day of the catheter insertion. PD dose was gradually increased to achieve the total dialysate volume of 20 L per day in the next few days. The delivered dose of PD by total weekly Kt/V and total weekly creatinine clearance (CCr) were 3.23 and 97.84 L/week, respectively. After a week of automated PD, nitrogen catabolites decreased gradually (BUN 27.8 mmol/L, Cr 840 µmol/L). At one month, his urine volume had increased to 1 L per day, but measured renal CCr was still at 4 mL/min/1.73 m2 which reflected inadequate recovery of renal function. He was discharged on day 31 of admission with continuation of automated PD at a total dialysate volume of 10 L per day. At follow-up visit, the patient showed gradually improvement in renal function and the dose of PD was tapered accordingly. Eventually, PD could be discontinued at 4 months after the onset of AKI. The patient attained stable serum Cr of 124 µmol/L and measured CCr of 29 mL/min/1.73 m2 afterwards.

Using the 2021 CKD-EPI Creatinine equation, what is the patient's Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR) in terms of mL/min/1.73 m²? You should use the patient's medical values and health status when they were first admitted to the hospital prior to any treatment.

step_info.args: {"focus": "CKD-EPI GFR for a 49-year-old male, Cr 1230 µmol/L, admitted with HIV and AKI. Prior to treatment baseline Cr 115 µmol/L."}

step_info.output:
Clinical Calculation Summary: CKD-EPI GFR 2021

1. Patient Data Extraction:
   - Age: 49
   - Sex: Male
   - Serum Creatinine (Cr): 1230 µmol/L (Converted to 13.91 mg/dL)
   - Note: Calculation based on acute admission creatinine.

2. Formula Used:
   CKD-EPI 2021 = 142 * min(Cr/k, 1)^a * max(Cr/k, 1)^(-1.200) * 0.9938^Age
   - Constants (Male): k = 0.9, a = -0.302

3. Step-by-Step Arithmetic:
   - Convert Cr: 1230 / 88.4 = 13.91 mg/dL
   - Calculate Ratio (Cr/k): 13.91 / 0.9 = 15.456
   - Apply Min Factor: min(15.456, 1)^(-0.302) = 1^(-0.302) = 1.0
   - Apply Max Factor: 15.456^(-1.200) ≈ 0.0384
   - Apply Age Factor: 0.9938^49 ≈ 0.7373
   - Final Product: 142 * 1.0 * 0.0384 * 0.7373

4. Final Result:
   Estimated GFR = 4.02 mL/min/1.73m²

step_info.thought: The patient's initial admission creatinine was 1,230 µmol/L at 49 years of age. Based on the 2021 CKD-EPI formula, the calculated GFR at the time of admission is 4.02 mL/min/1.73 m².

step_info.output: Final result processed.

calculator_name: HOMA-IR (Homeostatic Model Assessment for Insulin Resistance)

args: A 46-year-old Dominican-American man came to the hospital’s pre-work sunrise laboratory session at 06:20 because a pop-up pharmacy that visited his construction site eight days earlier recorded a capillary glucose “a shade over 120” and advised him to secure a formal fasting specimen before he contemplated cutting out rice, purchasing over-the-counter cinnamon capsules, or accepting co-workers’ spare metformin tablets. He intentionally kept his behavior unchanged during the intervening week so that today’s data would depict his usual physiology: Monday through Saturday he rises at 04:30, drives forty minutes to a municipal bridge-repair project, and spends ten hours directing jackhammer crews from a supervisor van where he snacks on empanadas, sweetened café con leche in a stainless thermos, and the mango soda stocked in the cooler. Lunch is often a styrofoam clamshell of white rice, stewed chicken, and fried plantains from a nearby food truck; dinner, served by his mother after evening traffic, is a plate piled with beans, yucca, and pork shoulder. Over the last four years his weight has increased from 74 kg to 88 kg at an unchanged height of 172 cm; he recently loosened his leather belt two notches and notices his reflective safety vest feels snug around the midriff by mid-afternoon. He reports voiding once during most nights, attributes that to finishing a 20-oz cola on the drive home, and denies relentless thirst, sudden weight loss, visual haze, cutaneous boils, or distal numbness. When a concrete delivery delay pushes lunch past 14:30 he experiences irritability, palmar tremor, and a subtle hollow ache beneath the sternum that resolve within minutes of the mango soda. Past medical history lists only seasonal rhinosinusitis treated intermittently with otc loratadine; no clinician has prescribed systemic glucocorticoids, antiretroviral agents, atypical antipsychotics, or any medication aimed at lowering plasma glucose, and he has not embarked on structured dieting apps, intermittent fasting, or gym memberships. Surgical history is limited to arthroscopic meniscus debridement at age 29. His father began “sugar pills” at 62 and now uses a basal analogue pen; his mother remains normoglycemic at 68. He smoked half a pack of cigarettes daily from ages 18 to 40 before quitting; he drinks one bottle of lager with Saturday dominoes and denies illicit substances. Physical activity outside of work is sparse because an old knee flares after prolonged kneeling, though he ambles around a neighborhood park with his teenage daughter on Sundays, logging roughly 5 000 steps by smart-phone pedometer. Review of systems is otherwise negative for exertional chest pressure, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, depressive mood, cold or heat intolerance, calf claudication, or change in shoe size. In the waiting area he appeared relaxed, scrolling sports scores while maintaining his fast. After five minutes of seated rest, nursing staff documented right-arm blood pressure 128/82 mm Hg, regular pulse 76 beats/min, respiratory rate 14/min, oral temperature 36.7 °C, and oxygen saturation 98 % breathing room air. Calibrated scale weight was 87.9 kg, yielding a body-mass index of 29.7 kg/m²; waist circumference measured 104 cm at the midpoint between the iliac crest and lowest rib. Posterior cervical inspection revealed a faint velvety hyperpigmented patch with three 2-mm skin tags; thyroid palpation was normal. Cardiovascular examination showed crisp S1 and S2 without gallops, murmurs, or rubs; dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses were 2+ and symmetric, and no lower-extremity edema was present. Lung fields were clear. The abdomen was rounded but soft and nontender, with no hepatosplenomegaly. Neurologic screen demonstrated intact cranial nerves, full strength, symmetric 2+ deep-tendon reflexes, preserved 10-g monofilament sensation on plantar surfaces, and vibration perception equal at halluces and medial malleoli. Ophthalmoscopic view revealed sharp disc margins without hemorrhages or exudates. He affirmed that after a last caloric intake at 18:50 yesterday—rice, red beans, and stewed goat—he consumed only tap water, specifically avoiding his customary predawn coffee. Venipuncture was performed at 06:28 into fluoride-oxalate and serum-separator tubes, placed on ice, and transported to the CAP-accredited core laboratory within eight minutes. The automated hexokinase analyzer reported a fasting plasma glucose concentration of 121 mg/dL, and the paired serum aliquot analyzed by electrochemiluminescent immunoassay demonstrated a fasting insulin level of 49 µIU/mL. Additional studies from the same specimen included hemoglobin A1c 5.8 %, serum creatinine 0.90 mg/dL with estimated glomerular filtration rate >90 mL/min/1.73 m², sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, chloride 103 mmol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 14 mg/dL, alanine aminotransferase 31 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 27 U/L, total cholesterol 209 mg/dL, low-density lipoprotein 134 mg/dL, high-density lipoprotein 46 mg/dL, triglycerides 176 mg/dL, thyroid-stimulating hormone 1.9 µIU/mL, and high-sensitivity C-reactive protein 2.9 mg/L. Urinalysis revealed specific gravity 1.012, trace protein, no glucose, and negative ketones; spot urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio measured 12 mg/g. A baseline 12-lead electrocardiogram obtained immediately before phlebotomy showed normal sinus rhythm at 74 beats/min without conduction delay or ischemic changes. The patient reiterated that he has not modified diet, activity, or taken any prescription or herbal product since the worksite alert; he seeks culturally appropriate meal planning that respects family gatherings, knee-sparing exercise strategies feasible around construction duties, and clarification regarding thresholds for pharmacologic intervention should lifestyle measures prove insufficient.
What is the patient's HOMA-IR score? You should use the patient's medical values and health status when they were first admitted to the hospital prior to any treatment.

step_info.args: {"focus": "Calculate HOMA-IR score using fasting glucose 121 mg/dL and fasting insulin 49 µIU/mL. The formula is (glucose in mg/dL * insulin in µIU/mL) / 405."}

step_info.output:
Clinical Calculation Summary: HOMA-IR Score

Extracted Variables:
- Fasting Glucose: 121 mg/dL
- Fasting Insulin: 49 µIU/mL

Formula Applied:
HOMA-IR = (Glucose * Insulin) / 405

Step-by-step Arithmetic:
1. Multiply glucose by insulin: 121 * 49 = 5929
2. Divide the result by 405: 5929 / 405 ≈ 14.64

Final Result:
14.64 (Unitless)

step_info.thought: The HOMA-IR calculation has been performed using the fasting glucose and insulin levels provided in the patient's note. The calculation resulted in a value of 14.64.

step_info.output: Final result processed.

args:
An eighty-five-year-old female presented to the emergency room with complaints of sudden onset substernal chest pain and dizziness. Her past medical history was significant for atrial fibrillation. She had a pacemaker inserted at another facility 2 weeks before. No perioperative complications were reported and patient had been doing well without any complaints till the day of presentation. Medications included dabigatran for her atrial fibrillation. On examination, the patient was diaphoretic but in no respiratory distress. Temperature was 97°F, pulse 96/minute, irregular; systolic blood pressure was 70 mm Hg; respiratory rate was 22/minute; and oxygen saturation was 95% on room air. No jugular venous distension was noted. Cardiac examination revealed irregular rhythm, no murmur or rub. Lung auscultation revealed slightly diminished breath sounds at left base. Chest X-ray showed a mild-to-moderate left-sided pleural effusion; pacemaker leads appeared to be in the right ventricle (). Electrocardiogram (EKG) showed trioventricular (AV) sequential pacing (). Echocardiogram (ECHO) revealed no wall motion abnormality with normal ejection fraction. Pacemaker leads were visualized in the right ventricle (RV) on ECHO. Her hemoglobin was 8 gm%; prothrombin time (PT) was 19 seconds; and INR was 2.9.
No pacing or sensing failure was noted on device interrogation. The patient's hypotension persisted despite vigorous intravenous fluid resuscitation including transfusion of 2 units of blood cell and 6 units of fresh frozen plasma and improvement in her hemoglobin to 11 gm%. A computed tomogram (CT) of the chest was performed which showed a moderate left pleural effusion, with the pacemaker wire extending to the left pleural cavity and a small localized pericardial effusion adjoining the pleural effusion (). The patient's hemodynamic instability was not due to tamponade effect as the pericardial effusion was small but due to continued hemorrhage into the pleural cavity due to her coagulopathic state from being on dabigatran for her atrial fibrillation, which was not fully corrected by fresh frozen plasma infusion.
The patient was taken to the operating room where she underwent a median sternotomy. The pacemaker lead was found to be protruding out of the right ventricle (). The external portion was cut with the remaining intracardiac portion of the pacemaker lead left in place. The perforation of the RV wall was repaired. The hemothorax and hemopericardium were evacuated. Subsequently, the patient's hemodynamic status improved. Two days later, the remaining portion of the pacemaker lead was removed transvenously. A new pacemaker was inserted transvenously and placed on the right ventricular septum. The patient did not have further complications and was discharged home subsequently. On follow-up 6 weeks later, she was doing well.

args:
RF is a 66-year-old male with a history of ischemic cardiomyopathy. The patient had a HeartMate II LVAD placed 3 years prior to presentation for medically refractory heart failure. He was on warfarin and aspirin for his LVAD with goal INR of 1.5–2.0. He presented to the emergency department with light-headedness and hematochezia for several days. He had no prior history of GIB. His physical examination and vital signs were unremarkable. His INR was 2.8, creatinine was 2.3 mg/dl, and hemoglobin was 10.4 from baseline of 12 to 13 g/dl. His last screening colonoscopy was 4 years prior and was reportedly unremarkable. He was admitted for close monitoring, serial laboratory testing, and gastroenterology consultation. He underwent esophagogastroduodenoscopy revealing a gastric ulcer with a clean base with no evidence of active bleeding. A colonoscopy revealed a likely malignant tumor in the cecum, diverticulosis, and nonbleeding colonic angiodysplastic lesions []. The cecal mass was biopsied; it appeared to be actively bleeding and uncontrollable endoscopically. These findings were relayed to the emergency general surgery team.
He was taken emergently to the operating room. A midline laparotomy was undertaken and he was found to have a cecal mass that was perforated into a retroperitoneal abscess. The right colon was mobilized to the hepatic flexure and the abscess cavity was debrided. A right colectomy was performed with a stapled side-to-side ileocolonic anastomosis. A drain was placed in the abscess cavity, fascia was closed, and the skin and subcutaneous tissues were packed with gauze.
Final pathology showed no evidence of malignancy. It revealed a transmural defect of 1.0 cm × 0.5 cm with organizing hematoma and acute on chronic inflammation. Cytomegalovirus testing was negative. There was focal re-epithelialization, thus making bleeding diverticulum a possible diagnosis.
The patient's postoperative course was complicated by prolonged ileus necessitating total parenteral nutrition and pelvic abscess that required percutaneous drain placement. The patient additionally had bacteremia and fungemia. The patient's postoperative complications eventually resolved, and he was discharged to home with home health.

Using the MDRD GFR Equation, what is the patient's Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR) in terms of mL/min/1.73 m²? If the patient is black, please use the MDRD GFR Equation for Blacks. step_info.output:

Formula Used:

Substitution: 
175 * (2.3)^(-1.154) * (66)^(-0.203)

Step-by-Step Arithmetic:
1. Calculate (2.3)^(-1.154) ≈ 0.3824
2. Calculate (66)^(-0.203) ≈ 0.4272
3. Multiply values: 175 * 0.3824 * 0.4272 ≈ 28.59

Final Result: 28.59 mL/min/1.73m²

step_info.thought: The calculation of the patient's GFR using the MDRD equation has been performed based on the initial clinical values identified in the patient note (age 66, creatinine 2.3 mg/dl, male, and not specified as black). The resulting GFR is 28.59 mL/min/1.73m². I have completed the necessary analysis and calculation and am ready to provide the final answer.

args: A 67-year-old right-handed man was brought to the internal medicine clinic by his spouse for progressive lower-extremity swelling and increasing exertional dyspnea over the past four weeks. He reports that during this period he has had to elevate his legs at night to keep the swelling down and sleeps on two pillows because lying flat leaves him short of breath; despite these changes he still awakens once or twice nightly feeling "waterlogged." He recalls being told eight years earlier that an echocardiogram showed weakening of the pumping function of his heart after an episode of viral myocarditis, and he has intermittently taken loop diuretics and an ACE inhibitor since then, though his adherence has been inconsistent. He has never experienced crushing substernal chest pain, nor has he undergone coronary angiography, and he specifically denies any previous heart attack, episodes of sudden diaphoresis with jaw or arm pain, or need for cardiac stenting or bypass. He likewise denies leg claudication, nonhealing foot ulcers, or prior vascular surgery, and states he has never been told he has blocked arteries in his legs. When questioned about neurologic events, he reports no sudden unilateral weakness, facial droop, slurred speech, or loss of vision, and there is no recollection of hospitalizations for stroke. His spouse confirms that his memory remains sharp; he manages the household finances and assists grandchildren with computer homework without difficulty, and she has noticed no personality change, disorientation, or repetitive questioning. He has never carried an inhaler, has never been hospitalized for bronchitis or emphysema, and is able to walk a city block without wheezing, though the ankle edema slows him now. His past gastrointestinal history is notable only for occasional dyspepsia relieved by over-the-counter antacids; he explicitly denies episodes of hematemesis, melena, or prior endoscopic documentation of ulcers. Of greater significance, he was diagnosed five years ago with biopsy-proven hepatitis C–related cirrhosis. He has esophageal varices seen on surveillance endoscopy and mild splenomegaly on abdominal ultrasound; his most recent laboratory studies from six months ago showed a total bilirubin of 3.1 mg/dL, INR 1.7, and albumin 2.8 g/dL. He has never developed overt hepatic encephalopathy or ascites requiring paracentesis, but he does note easy bruising and sporadic epistaxis. Despite this chronic liver condition, he has never been found to have any solid tumors and has no family history of gastrointestinal or hepatic malignancy; routine age-appropriate colonoscopy two years ago was unremarkable. He has never received chemotherapy, radiation, or bone marrow-suppressive agents and has not been told of any abnormal white blood cell proliferation; complete blood counts in prior records show no evidence of leukocytosis or lymphadenopathy, and he has not experienced night sweats, drenching fevers, or unexplained weight loss. He has maintained normal fasting and postprandial glucose values on annual screening, and lifestyle counseling alone—primarily focusing on portion control and walking after meals—has sufficed to keep his hemoglobin A1c below 6%; he takes no oral hypoglycemics or insulin. Renal function has historically been normal with a baseline creatinine of 0.9 mg/dL, and urine microalbumin checks have been negative. Physical examination today finds an alert, oriented gentleman in no acute distress, speaking full sentences. Blood pressure is 124/72 mm Hg, heart rate 88 beats per minute and regular, respiratory rate 18, temperature 36.8 °C, and oxygen saturation 97% on room air. Jugular venous distention is present at 9 cm H₂O. The point of maximal impulse is laterally displaced; S1 and S2 are audible with a soft S3 gallop and no murmurs. Bibasilar crackles are absent; breath sounds are vesicular throughout. The abdomen is scaphoid with a palpable left-sided liver edge that is firm and non-tender; no fluid wave is noted. There is pitting edema to the mid-shins bilaterally, without erythema or tenderness, and distal pulses are symmetric and strong. Neurologic examination reveals intact cranial nerves, full strength in all extremities, and normal sensation; gait is steady. The patient walks unassisted to the exam table and recalls three objects at five minutes. Initial assessment is decompensation of previously known systolic dysfunction in the setting of advanced chronic liver disease, with no evidence of ischemic heart disease, peripheral vascular occlusive disease, cerebrovascular insult, chronic lung pathology, peptic ulceration, diabetes requiring pharmacotherapy, renal impairment, or malignancy.
What is the patient's Charlson Comorbidity Index (CCI)? You should use the patient's medical values and health status when they were first admitted to the hospital prior to any treatment.

args: A 41-year-old woman who works as a school librarian was driven to the emergency department by her partner in the mid-afternoon after two days of progressively worsening dry cough that became productive of scant rust-tinged sputum overnight, new pleuritic discomfort along the left posterior axillary line, and a low but persistent pressure sensation in the temples whenever she took a deep breath. Earlier in the day she measured an oral temperature of 101.1 °F with a home thermometer and noted that her smartwatch alarmed for a “high heart rate” while she sat at her desk cataloguing books. She denies syncope, faintness on standing, or confusion; she recalls the date, morning news headlines, and the full route taken to the hospital without hesitation. Past medical history is notable for dilated cardiomyopathy of presumed viral etiology diagnosed five years ago with a last documented left-ventricular ejection fraction of 35 % and New York Heart Association class II symptoms well-controlled on carvedilol, low-dose sacubitril–valsartan, and a daily 20 mg furosemide that she admits she skipped yesterday because she “didn’t want to run to the restroom during classes.” She is also followed in hepatology clinic for autoimmune hepatitis leading to early fibrosis; transaminases have been chronically two to three times normal but albumin and INR have remained intact, and she has never required lactulose, paracentesis, or diuretics for fluid accumulation. She has no known kidney disease, her most recent outpatient creatinine three months ago was 0.7 mg/dL with a urea nitrogen of 16 mg/dL, and she has never been hospitalized for renal concerns. There is no personal history of malignancy, stroke, peripheral vascular events, or chronic neurologic deficits. She quit a modest five–pack-year smoking habit ten years earlier, drinks an occasional cider on weekends, and denies recreational drug use. Vaccinations include last autumn’s influenza shot and a conjugate pneumococcal dose administered two years ago. She took no antipyretics, antibiotics, inhalers, or supplemental oxygen prior to arrival. Emergency medical services placed her on a cardiac monitor but delivered no intravenous fluids or medications en route so that initial physiology could be documented without influence. On presentation she sat upright in bed, speaking in full sentences, skin warm and lightly diaphoretic, mucous membranes dry, and capillary refill brisk; jugular venous pulsation was at 4 cm above the sternal angle and trace symmetric ankle edema present, findings identical to her cardiology baseline. Triage vital signs obtained before any therapy revealed oral temperature 101.2 °F, regular tachycardic pulse at 116 beats min⁻¹, automated blood pressure 127/77 mm Hg confirmed manually, respiratory rate 22 breaths min⁻¹ with mild subcostal retractions, and fingertip pulse oximetry fluctuating between 88 % and 90 % on ambient air at sea-level altitude. Cardiac auscultation disclosed a rapid S1 and S2 with an occasional third heart sound but no murmurs or rubs. Pulmonary examination showed decreased breath sounds and dullness to percussion over the inferior half of the left posterior lung field with a few coarse crackles just above that zone; the right lung transmitted vesicular sounds throughout without adventitious noises. Abdomen was soft; a smooth liver edge was palpable 1 cm below the costal margin but nontender, and there was no splenomegaly or ascites. Neurologic screen revealed equal reactive pupils, intact extra-ocular movements, normal speech cadence, and 5/5 strength throughout with steady rapid alternating movements. Bedside capillary glucose registered 121 mg/dL. Venous blood drawn within ten minutes of arrival and before any IV flushes demonstrated sodium 116 mEq/L, potassium 3.6 mEq/L, chloride 92 mEq/L, bicarbonate 23 mEq/L, blood urea nitrogen 23 mg/dL, creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, and hematocrit 36 %. A radial arterial sample obtained on room air immediately thereafter showed pH 7.61, PaCO₂ 28 mm Hg, PaO₂ 56 mm Hg, and calculated bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, indicating primary respiratory alkalosis with significant hypoxemia. White-cell count returned at 13.8 × 10⁹ L⁻¹ with 85 % neutrophils; platelet count was 210 × 10⁹ L⁻¹. A portable postero-anterior chest radiograph performed before any medications or fluids displayed a segmental consolidation in the left lower lobe accompanied by a blunted costophrenic angle and a thin layering density consistent with a small free-flowing pleural fluid collection; the cardiac silhouette was moderately enlarged compared with earlier images but unchanged from her baseline echocardiographic report. Electrocardiography showed sinus tachycardia at 115 beats min⁻¹ with nonspecific lateral T-wave flattening, no acute ischemic changes, and QRS duration identical to prior tracings. Two sets of peripheral blood cultures, an expectorated sputum sample for Gram stain and culture, urinary antigens for common bacterial pathogens, and a multiplex respiratory viral panel were sent before any antimicrobial orders were placed. At the moment this narrative is entered, the patient remains seated upright breathing room air, has received no intravenous crystalloid aside from the small heparinized arterial flush, no diuretic, vasodilator, opioid, bronchodilator, or antipyretic, and no supplemental oxygen, thereby preserving the physiologic and laboratory data above as her untreated state at initial presentation.
